definitions.source_name: NCSC UK Glossary
definitions.definition: Recently discovered vulnerability that hackers can explot, where a fix or patch isn't publicly available yet.

definitions.source_name: INCIBE Glosario de terminos de ciberseguridad
definitions.definition: Son aquellas vulnerabilidades en sistemas o programas informáticos que son únicamente conocidas por determinados atacantes y son desconocidas por los fabricantes y usuarios. Al ser desconocidas por los fabricantes, no existe un parche de seguridad para solucionarlas. Por esta razón son muy peligrosas ya que el atacante puede explotarlas sin que el usuario sea consciente de que es vulnerable. Sinónimo: 0-day

definitions.source_name: Texas DIR Cybersecurity Terminology
definitions.definition: A zero-day vulnerability is a computer-software vulnerability that is unknown to those who would be interested in mitigating the vulnerability. Until the vulnerability is mitigated, hackers can exploit it to adversely affect computer programs, data, additional computers or a network.

definitions.source_name: Government of Canada Enterprise Cyber Security Strategy — Glossary
definitions.definition: An attack directed against a zero-day vulnerability.

definitions.source_name: Glossário de Segurança da Informação (GSI/PR Brazil)
definitions.definition: vide exploração de dia zero;

definitions.source_name: Washington State AG Data Breach Glossary
definitions.definition: A cyberattack that occurs on the same day a weakness is discovered in software , before the software developer has a chance to fix it.

definitions.source_name: Glossário de Segurança da Informação (GSI/PR Brazil)
definitions.definition: sigla de autenticação de dois fatores (2 factor authentication ).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Authentication using two or more factors to achieve authentication. Factors include: (i) something you know (e.g., password/personal identification number [PIN]); (ii) something you have (e.g., cryptographic identification device, token); or (iii) something you are (e.g., biometric).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: View of an object that focuses on the information relevant to a particular purpose and ignores the remainder of the information.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An authentication system that requires more than one distinct authentication factor for successful authentication. Multifactor authentication can be performed using a multifactor authenticator or by a combination of authenticators that provide different factors. The three authentication factors are something you know, something you have, and something you are.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Authentication using two or more different factors to achieve authentication. Factors include: (i) something you know (e.g., password/PIN); (ii) something you have (e.g., cryptographic identification device, token); or (iii) something you are (e.g., biometric). See Authenticator.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (D) See: Deprecated Usage under "attribute authority".

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An entity, recognized by the Federal PKI Policy Authority or comparable Agency body as having the authority to verify the association of attributes to an identity.

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: See additional authenticated data .

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The input data to the authenticated encryption function that is authenticated but not encrypted.

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: Authenticator Assurance Level

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A list of applications and application components (libraries, configuration files, etc.) that are authorized to be present or active on a host according to a well-defined baseline.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A category describing the strength of the authentication process.

definitions.source_name: ENISA Glossary of Terms
definitions.definition: Accrual-based accounting

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An access control approach in which access is mediated based on attributes associated with subjects (requesters) and the objects to be accessed. Each object and subject has a set of associated attributes, such as location, time of creation, access rights, etc. Access to an object is authorized or denied depending upon whether the required (e.g., policy-defined) correlation can be made between the attributes of that object and of the requesting subject.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Access control based on attributes associated with and about subjects, objects, targets, initiators, resources, or the environment. An access control rule set defines the combination of attributes under which an access may take place. See also identity, credential, and access management (ICAM).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: an access control paradigm whereby access rights are granted to users through the use of policies which combine attributes together. The policies can use any type of attributes (user attributes, resource attributes, environment attribute etc.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: High-level requirements that specify how access is managed and who may access information under what circumstances.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The set of rules that define the conditions under which an access may take place.

definitions.source_name: Glossário de Segurança da Informação (GSI/PR Brazil)
definitions.definition: sigla de autoridade certificadora;

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of granting or denying specific requests to 1) obtain and use information and related information processing services and 2) enter specific physical facilities (e.g., federal buildings, military establishments, border crossing entrances).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The decision to permit or deny a subject access to system objects (network, data, application, service, etc.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: To ensure that an entity can only access protected resources if they have the appropriate permissions based on the predefined access control policies.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of granting or denying specific requests to: 1) obtain and use information and related information processing services; and 2) enter specific physical facilities (e.g., federal buildings, military establishments, border crossing entrances).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of permitting or restricting access to applications at a granular level, such as per-user, per-group, and per-resources.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Procedures and controls that limit or detect access to critical information resources. This can be accomplished through software, biometrics devices, or physical access to a controlled space.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Enable authorized use of a resource while preventing unauthorized use or use in an unauthorized manner.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Process of granting access to information system resources only to authorized users, programs, processes, or other systems.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of granting access to information technology (IT) system resources only to authorized users, programs, processes, or other systems.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of granting or denying specific requests to: (i) obtain and use information and related information processing services; and (ii) enter specific physical facilities (e.g., Federal buildings, military establishments, and border-crossing entrances).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of granting or denying specific requests for obtaining and using information and related information processing services; and to enter specific physical facilities (e.g., Federal buildings, military establishments, and border crossing entrances).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of limiting access to resources of a system only to authorized programs, processes, or other systems (in a network).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of granting or denying specific requests for obtaining and using information and related information processing services.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of granting or denying specific requests: 1) obtain and use information and related information processing services; and 2) enter specific physical facilities (e.g., Federal buildings, military establishments, border crossing entrances).

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
1a. (I) The ability and means to communicate with or otherwise
interact with a system to use system resources either to handle
information or to gain knowledge of the information the system
contains. (Compare: handle.)

Usage: The definition is intended to include all types of
communication with a system, including one-way communication in
either direction. In actual practice, however, passive users might
be treated as not having "access" and, therefore, be exempt from
most requirements of the system's security policy. (See: "passive
user" under "user".)

1b. (O) "Opportunity to make use of an information system (IS)
resource." [C4009]

2. (O) /formal model/ "A specific type of interaction between a
subject and an object that results in the flow of information from
one to the other." [NCS04]

definitions.source_name: NICCS Cybersecurity Glossary (CISA)
definitions.definition: The ability and means to communicate with or otherwise interact with a system, to use system resources to handle information, to gain knowledge of the information the system contains, or to control system components and functions.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) /information system/ A mechanism that implements access
control for a system resource by enumerating the system entities
that are permitted to access the resource and stating, either
implicitly or explicitly, the access modes granted to each entity.
(Compare: access control matrix, access list, access profile,
capability list.)

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: A document that defines who can access a particular bucket or object. Each bucket and object in Amazon S3 has an ACL. This document defines what each type of user can do, such as write and read permissions.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) A rectangular array of cells, with one row per subject and one
column per object. The entry in a cell -- that is, the entry for a
particular subject-object pair -- indicates the access mode that
the subject is permitted to exercise on the object. Each column is

equivalent to an "access control list" for the object; and each
row is equivalent to an "access profile" for the subject.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A table in which each row represents a subject, each column represents an object, and each entry is the set of access rights for that subject to that object.

definitions.source_name: NICCS Cybersecurity Glossary (CISA)
definitions.definition: Security measures designed to detect and deny unauthorized access and permit authorized access to an information system or a physical facility.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Implementations of formal AC policy such as AC model. Access control mechanisms can be designed to adhere to the properties of the model by machine implementation using protocols, architecture, or formal languages such as program code.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Security safeguards (i.e., hardware and software features, physical controls, operating procedures, management procedures, and various combinations of these) designed to detect and deny unauthorized access and permit authorized access to an information system.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
1. (D) Synonym for the hierarchical "classification level" in a
security level. [C4009] (See: security level.)

2. (D) Synonym for "clearance level".

Deprecated Definitions: IDOCs SHOULD NOT use this term with these
definitions because they duplicate the meaning of more specific
terms. Any IDOC that uses this term SHOULD provide a specific
definition for it because access control may be based on many
attributes other than classification level and clearance level.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A category within a given security classification limiting entry or system connectivity to only authorized persons.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) /physical security/ Roster of persons who are authorized to
enter a controlled area. (Compare: access control list.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A list of users, programs, and/or processes and the specifications of access categories to which each is assigned.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Roster of individuals authorized admittance to a controlled area.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(O) Synonym for "capability list".

Usage: IDOCs that use this term SHOULD state a definition for it
because the definition is not widely known.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Association of a user with a list of protected objects the user may access.

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: A formal relationship with AWS that's associated with all of the following: The owner email address and password The control of resources created under its umbrella Payment for the AWS activity related to those resources The AWS account has permission to do anything and everything with all the AWS account resources. This is in contrast to a user , which is an entity contained within the account.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An entity in a blockchain that is identified with an address and can send transactions to the blockchain.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) The property of a system or system resource that ensures that
the actions of a system entity may be traced uniquely to that
entity, which can then be held responsible for its actions. [Huff]
(See: audit service.)

Tutorial: Accountability (a.k.a. individual accountability)
typically requires a system ability to positively associate the
identity of a user with the time, method, and mode of the user's
access to the system. This ability supports detection and
subsequent investigation of security breaches. Individual persons
who are system users are held accountable for their actions after
being notified of the rules of behavior for using the system and
the penalties associated with violating those rules.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Property that ensures that the actions of an entity may be traced uniquely to the entity.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A privacy principle (FIPP) that refers to an organization's requirements to demonstrate their implementation of the FIPPs and applicable privacy requirements.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The principle that an individual is entrusted to safeguard and control equipment, keying material, and information and is answerable to proper authority for the loss or misuse of that equipment or information.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The security goal that generates the requirement for actions of an entity to be traced uniquely to that entity. This supports non-repudiation, deterrence, fault isolation, intrusion detection and prevention, and after-action recovery and legal action.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A property that ensures that the actions of an entity may be traced uniquely to that entity.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The security objective that generates the requirement for actions of an entity to be traced uniquely to that entity. This supports non-repudiation, deterrence, fault isolation, intrusion detection and prevention, and after-action recovery and legal action.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 2. The security goal that generates the requirement for actions of an entity to be traced uniquely to that entity. This supports non-repudiation, deterrence, fault isolation, intrusion detection and prevention, and after-action recovery and legal action.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 1. Assigning key management responsibilities to individuals and holding them accountable for these activities. 2. A property that ensures that the actions of an entity may be traced uniquely to that entity.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The property that ensures that the actions of an entity may be traced uniquely to the entity.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The property of being able to trace activities on a system to individuals who may then be held responsible for their actions.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (O) Synonym for "security perimeter". [C4009]

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Identifies the information resources covered by an accreditation decision, as distinguished from separately accredited information resources that are interconnected or with which information is exchanged via messaging.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: For the purposes of identifying the Protection Level for confidentiality of a system to be accredited, the system has a conceptual boundary that extends to all intended users of the system, both directly and indirectly connected, who receive output from the system.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: All components of an information system to be accredited by an authorizing official and excludes separately accredited systems, to which the information system is connected. Synonymous with the term security perimeter defined in CNSS Instruction 4009 and DCID 6/3.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: All components of an information system to be accredited by an authorizing official and excludes separately accredited systems to which the information system is connected. Synonymous with the term security perimeter defined in CNSS Instruction 4009 and DCID 6/3.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Product comprised of a system security plan (SSP) and a report documenting the basis for the accreditation decision. Rationale: The RMF uses a new term to refer to this concept, and it is called RMF security authorization package.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A physical or logical boundary that is defined for a system, domain, or enclave; within which a particular security policy or security architecture is applied.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See Accreditation Boundary.

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: AWS Certificate Manager is a web service for provisioning, managing, and deploying Secure Sockets Layer/ Transport Layer Security (SSL/TLS) certificates for use with AWS services. See also https://aws.amazon.com/certificate-manager/ .

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Implementations of formal AC policy such as AC model. Access control mechanisms can be designed to adhere to the properties of the model by machine implementation using protocols, architecture, or formal languages such as program code.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
1. (O) /SET/ "The financial institution that establishes an
account with a merchant and processes payment card authorizations
and payments." [SET1]

2. (O) /SET/ "The institution (or its agent) that acquires from
the card acceptor the financial data relating to the transaction
and initiates that data into an interchange system." [SET2]

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Organization or entity that acquires or procures a product or service.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Stakeholder that acquires or procures a product or service.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Stakeholder that acquires or procures a product or service from a supplier.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(N) Secret data, other than keys, that is required to access a
cryptographic module. (See: CIK. Compare: initialization value.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A pass-phrase, personal identification number (PIN), biometric data, or other mechanisms of equivalent authentication robustness used to protect access to any use of a private key, except for private keys associated with System or Device certificates.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Private data, other than keys, that are required to access cryptographic modules (i.e., unlock private keys for signing or decryption events).

definitions.source_name: NCSC UK Glossary
definitions.definition: A set of NCSC services that are designed to reduce high-volume commodity cyber attacks.

definitions.source_name: Cyber Law Toolkit Glossary
definitions.definition: The taking of proactive defensive measures outside the defended cyber infrastructure. A hack-back is a type of active cyber defence.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Set of cohesive tasks of a process.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: 1. An activity is generally the smallest portion of a project used in planning, tracking, and control. In some projects, activities may be referred to as tasks, stories, work packages, or use cases, or using other descriptors. 2. Set of cohesive tasks of a process. 1. Activity Definition (PMBOK® Guide 6.2) - The Project Management Tool Kit, 3rd Edition [Book] (oreilly.com) 2. NIST SP 800-160v1r1 from ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288:2015

definitions.source_name: CWE Glossary (also CAPEC, CAR)
definitions.definition: a vulnerability theory term that describes an entity that interacts with a product or with other entities, such as a User, Service, Monitor (e.g. IDS), Intermediary, and others.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The source of risk that can result in harmful impact.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See threat actor.

definitions.source_name: ENISA Glossary of Terms
definitions.definition: Administrator

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Input data to the CCM generation-encryption process that is authenticated but not encrypted.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A Microsoft directory service for the management of identities in Windows domain networks.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(N) The retrofitting of protection mechanisms, implemented by
hardware or software, in an information system after the system
has become operational. [FP039] (Compare: baked-in security.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Incorporation of new or additional hardware, software, or firmware safeguards in an operational information system.

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: Information that's checked for integrity but not encrypted, such as headers or other contextual metadata.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The input data to the authenticated encryption function that is authenticated but not encrypted.

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: The official location where an individual can be found that is on record with a trusted or authoritative entity such as a government agency, the individual’s employer, financial institution, or utility company. The address of record always includes the residential street address of an individual and may also include the mailing address of the individual.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The validated and verified location (physical or digital) where an individual can receive communications using approved mechanisms.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The official location where an individual can be found. The address of record always includes the residential street address of an individual and may also include the mailing address of the individual. In very limited circumstances, an Army Post Office box number, Fleet Post Office box number or the street address of next of kin or of another contact individual can be used when a residential street address for the individual is not available.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(O) /U.S. DoD/ "Security commensurate with the risk and magnitude
of harm resulting from the loss, misuse, or unauthorized access to
or modification of information." (See: acceptable risk, residual
risk.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Meets minimum tolerable levels of security as determined by analysis, experience, or a combination of both and is as secure as reasonably practicable (i.e., incremental improvement in security would require an intolerable or disproportionate deterioration of meeting other system objectives, such as those for system performance, or would violate system constraints).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Security commensurate with the risk and the magnitude of harm resulting from the loss, misuse, or unauthorized access to or modification of information.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Security commensurate with the risk and magnitude of harm resulting from the loss, misuse, or unauthorized access to or modification of information.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Security commensurate with the risk resulting from the loss, misuse, or unauthorized access to or modification of information.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: security commensurate with the risk and magnitude of the harmresulting from the loss, misuse, or unauthorized access to or modification of information. This includes assuring that systems and applications operate effectively and provide appropriate confidentiality, integrity, and availability, through the use of cost-effective management, acquisition, development, installation, operational, and technical controls.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Security protections commensurate with the risk resulting from the unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction of information. This includes ensuring that information hosted on behalf of an agency and information systems and applications used by the agency operate effectively and provide appropriate confidentiality, integrity, and availability protections through the application of cost-effective security controls.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Security commensurate with the risk and the magnitude of harm resulting from the loss, misuse, or unauthorized access to or modification of information. This includes assuring that systems and applications used by the agency operate effectively and provide appropriate confidentiality, integrity, and availability, through the use of cost-effective management, personnel, operational, and technical controls.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
1. (O) /Common Criteria/ A person that is responsible for
configuring, maintaining, and administering the TOE in a correct
manner for maximum security. (See: administrative security.)

2. (O) /ITSEC/ A person in contact with the TOE, who is
responsible for maintaining its operational capability.

definitions.source_name: NCSC UK Glossary
definitions.definition: A person who manages a computer system, usually with highly privileged access to data and services.

definitions.source_name: Glossário de Segurança da Informação (GSI/PR Brazil)
definitions.definition: (AES) - criado pelo Instituto Nacional de Padrões e Tecnologia (NIST), tornou-se o padrão efetivo do governo federal americano em 2002, após cinco anos de desenvolvimento. Esse desenvolvimento começou em 1997, quando se verificou que seu antecessor, o data encryption standard (criptografia de dados padrão - DES), não estava mais atendia os critérios de segurança. O AES é construído a partir de três cifras de bloco: AES-128, AES-192 e AES-256. Cada uma dessas criptografa e decriptografa os dados em pedaços de 128 bits, usando chaves criptográficas de 128, 192 ou 256 bits. As chaves de 128 bits têm 10 rodadas de processamento, as chaves de 192 bits têm 12 e as de 256 bits 14 rodadas;

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A U.S. Government-approved cryptographic algorithm that can be used to protect electronic data. The AES algorithm is a symmetric block cipher that can encrypt (encipher) and decrypt (decipher) information.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Advanced Encryption Standard (as specified in FIPS 197).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Advanced Encryption Standard specified in [FIPS 197].

definitions.source_name: NICCS Cybersecurity Glossary (CISA)
definitions.definition: An adversary that possesses sophisticated levels of expertise and significant resources which allow it to create opportunities to achieve its objectives by using multiple attack vectors (e.g., cyber, physical, and deception).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An adversary that possesses sophisticated levels of expertise and significant resources that allow it to create opportunities to achieve its objectives by using multiple attack vectors including, for example, cyber, physical, and deception. These objectives typically include establishing and extending footholds within the IT infrastructure of the targeted organizations for purposes of exfiltrating information, undermining or impeding critical aspects of a mission, program, or organization, or positioning itself to carry out these objectives in the future. The advanced persistent threat pursues its objectives repeatedly over an extended period; adapts to defenders’ efforts to resist it; and is determined to maintain the level of interaction needed to execute its objectives.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An adversary that possesses sophisticated levels of expertise and significant resources which allow it to create opportunities to achieve its objectives by using multiple attack vectors (e.g., cyber, physical, and deception). These objectives typically include establishing and extending footholds within the information technology infrastructure of the targeted organizations for purposes of exfiltrating information, undermining or impeding critical aspects of a mission, program, or organization; or positioning itself to carry out these objectives in the future. The advanced persistent threat: (i) pursues its objectives repeatedly over an extended period of time; (ii) adapts to defenders' efforts to resist it; and (iii) is determined to maintain the level of interaction needed to execute its objectives.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An adversary with sophisticated levels of expertise and significant resources, allowing it through the use of multiple different attack vectors (e.g., cyber, physical, and deception), to generate opportunities to achieve its objectives which are typically to establish and extend its presence within the information technology infrastructure of organizations for purposes of continually exfiltrating information and/or to undermine or impede critical aspects of a mission, program, or organization, or place itself in a position to do so in the future; moreover, the advanced persistent threat pursues its objectives repeatedly over an extended period of time, adapting to a defender’s efforts to resist it, and with determination to maintain the level of interaction needed to execute its objectives.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An adversary that possesses sophisticated levels of expertise and significant resources which allow it to create opportunities to achieve its objectives by using multiple attack vectors including, for example, cyber, physical, and deception. These objectives typically include establishing and extending footholds within the IT infrastructure of the targeted organizations for purposes of exfiltrating information, undermining or impeding critical aspects of a mission, program, or organization; or positioning itself to carry out these objectives in the future. The advanced persistent threat pursues its objectives repeatedly over an extended period; adapts to defenders’ efforts to resist it; and is determined to maintain the level of interaction needed to execute its objectives.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An adversary that possesses sophisticated levels of expertise and significant resources which allow it to create opportunities to achieve its objectives by using multiple attack vectors (e.g., cyber, physical, and deception). These objectives typically include establishing and extending footholds within the information technology infrastructure of the targeted organizations for purposes of exfiltrating information, undermining or impeding critical aspects of a mission, program, or organization; or positioning itself to carry out these objectives in the future. The advanced persistent threat: (i) pursues its objectives repeatedly over an extended period of time; (ii) adapts to defenders’ efforts to resist it; and (iii) is determined to maintain the level of interaction needed to execute its objectives.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An adversary that possesses sophisticated levels of expertise and significant resources which allow it to create opportunities to achieve its objectives by using multiple attack vectors including, for example, cyber, physical, and deception. These objectives typically include establishing and extending footholds within the IT infrastructure of the targeted organizations for purposes of exfiltrating information, undermining or impeding critical aspects of a mission, program, or organization, or positioning itself to carry out these objectives in the future. The advanced persistent threat pursues its objectives repeatedly over an extended period; adapts to defenders’ efforts to resist it; and is determined to maintain the level of interaction needed to execute its objectives.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An adversary that possesses sophisticated levels of expertise and significant resources which allow it to create opportunities to achieve its objectives by using multiple attack vectors, including cyber, physical, and deception. These objectives typically include establishing and extending footholds within the IT infrastructure of the targeted organizations for purposes of exfiltrating information, undermining or impeding critical aspects of a mission, program, or organization; or positioning itself to carry out these objectives in the future. The advanced persistent threat pursues its objectives repeatedly over an extended period; adapts to defenders’ efforts to resist it; and is determined to maintain the level of interaction needed to execute its objectives.

definitions.source_name: Washington State AG Data Breach Glossary
definitions.definition: A prolonged and targeted cyberattack in which an intruder gains access to a network and remains undetected for an extended period.

definitions.source_name: Texas DIR Cybersecurity Terminology
definitions.definition: Targeted attacks differ from APTs in the same way a handgun differs from a state-of-the-art military issued rifle: sophistication, engineering, and user.  APTs are attacks that use code and tools that have been designed from the group up by groups of well-talented salaried engineers.  APTs are also state-sponsored attacks—which means that actual governments are behind them, rather than just a small group of hackers as is in the case of targeted attacks.  APTs are much more serious in scope and firepower than targeted attacks, and typically only go after big targets like government agencies.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) A circumstance in which a collection of information items is
required to be classified at a higher security level than any of
the items is classified individually. (See: classification.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See “Event Aggregation”.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The consolidation of similar log entries into a single entry containing a count of the number of occurrences of the event.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The consolidation of similar or related information.

definitions.source_name: NCSC UK Glossary
definitions.definition: A security strategy organisations can use that approves a list of email addresses, IP addresses, domain names or applications, while denying all others, to protect computers and networks from threats.

definitions.source_name: Canadian Centre for Cyber Security Glossary
definitions.definition: An access control list that identifies who or what is allowed access, in order to provide protection from harm.

definitions.source_name: NICCS Cybersecurity Glossary (CISA)
definitions.definition: An allowlist (formerly whitelist) is a cybersecurity strategy that explicitly permits specific, trusted entities—such as applications, IP addresses, or websites—to access a system or network, while denying all others. It enhances security by enforcing a "deny-by-default" policy, preventing unauthorized software or traffic from running

definitions.source_name: CWE Glossary (also CAPEC, CAR)
definitions.definition: a list or group of characters, strings, patterns, or other elements that is used to identify whether certain data should be allowed or accepted; treated as safe; included; or processed without modification.

normalized_term: american standard code for information interchange (ascii

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.term: American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ASCII)
definitions.definition:
(N) A scheme that encodes 128 specified characters -- the numbers
0-9, the letters a-z and A-Z, some basic punctuation symbols, some
control codes that originated with Teletype machines, and a blank
space -- into the 7-bit binary integers. Forms the basis of the
character set representations used in most computers and many
Internet standards. [FP001] (See: code.)

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.term: American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ASCII)
definitions.definition: ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) is a standard character encoding used in telecommunication. The ASCII pronounced 'ask-ee', is strictly a seven-bit code based on the English alphabet. ASCII codes are used to represent alphanumeric data. The code was first published as a standard in 1967. it was subsequently updated and published as ANSI X3.4-1968, then as ANSI X3.4-1977, and finally as ANSI X3.4-1986. Since it is a seven-bit code, it can at the most represent 128 characters. it currently defines 95 printable characters including 26 upper case letters (A to Z) , 26 lower case letters , 10 numerals (0 to 9) , and 33 special characters including mathematical symbols , punctuation marks , and space characters . They represent text in, telecommunications equipment, and devices. These include numbers, upper and lowercase English letters, functions, punctuation symbols, and some other symbols. In total, there are 256 ASCII characters, and can be broadly divided into three categories:

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The examination of acquired data for its significance and probative value to the case.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The third phase of the computer and network forensic process, which involves using legally justifiable methods and techniques, to derive useful information that addresses the questions that were the impetus for performing the collection and examination.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: The detailed study and examination of something, in order to discover more about it ( from Cambridge International Dictionary of English ). Analysis typically includes discovering parts of the item being studied, as well as how they fit together. An example is the study of schedule variances for cause, impact, corrective action, and results.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) The condition of an identity being unknown or concealed. (See:
alias, anonymizer, anonymous credential, anonymous login,
identity, onion routing, persona certificate. Compare: privacy.)

Tutorial: An application may require security services that
maintain anonymity of users or other system entities, perhaps to
preserve their privacy or hide them from attack. To hide an
entity's real name, an alias may be used; for example, a financial
institution may assign account numbers. Parties to transactions
can thus remain relatively anonymous, but can also accept the
transactions as legitimate. Real names of the parties cannot be
easily determined by observers of the transactions, but an
authorized third party may be able to map an alias to a real name,
such as by presenting the institution with a court order. In other
applications, anonymous entities may be completely untraceable.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Condition in identification whereby an entity can be recognized as distinct, without sufficient identity information to establish a link to a known identity.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: condition in identification whereby an entity can be recognized as distinct, without sufficient identity information to establish a link to a known identity

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (N) See: American National Standards Institute.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: A voluntary non-profit organization that administers, coordinates, and supports the U.S. voluntary consensus standards and conformity assessment system.

definitions.source_name: NICCS Cybersecurity Glossary (CISA)
definitions.definition: Anti-forensics refers to techniques designed to deliberately hinder, confuse, or destroy digital evidence, making forensic investigation difficult or impossible.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A technique for concealing or destroying data so that others cannot access it.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(N) "Measures ensuring that transmitted information can be
received despite deliberate jamming attempts." [C4009] (See:
electronic security, frequency hopping, jam, spread spectrum.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The result of measures to resist attempts to interfere with communications reception.

definitions.source_name: NCSC UK Glossary
definitions.definition: Software that is designed to detect, stop and remove viruses and other kinds of malicious software.

definitions.source_name: INCIBE Glosario de terminos de ciberseguridad
definitions.definition:
Software de protección para evitar que ejecutemos algún tipo de software malicioso en nuestro equipo que infecte al equipo.

Sinónimo: Antimalware

definitions.source_name: NICCS Cybersecurity Glossary (CISA)
definitions.definition: Antivirus software is a security program designed to detect, prevent, and remove malicious software (malware)—including viruses, ransomware, spyware, and Trojans—from computers, laptops, and mobile devices.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A program specifically designed to detect many forms of malware and prevent them from infecting computers, as well as cleaning computers that have already been infected.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A program that monitors a computer or network to identify all major types of malware and prevent or contain malware incidents.

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: See "Access Point"

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A set of filter processes that are arranged in a linear order using one-way inter-process communications to transfer data between processes. The linear flow is enforced with mandatory and discretionary access control mechanisms.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: See: Internet Protocol Suite, OSIRM.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Layer of the TCP/IP protocol stack that sends and receives data for particular applications such as DNS, HTTP, and SMTP.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A system access point or library function that has a well-defined syntax and is accessible from application programs or user code to provide well-defined functionality.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: An interface that a software program implements in order to allow other software to interact with it, much in the same way that software might implement a user interface in order to allow humans to use it. APIs are implemented by applications, libraries and operating systems to define how other software can make calls to or request services from them.[1][2][3] An API determines the vocabulary and calling conventions the programmer should employ to use the services. It may include specifications for routines, data structures, object classes, and protocols used to communicate between the consumer and implementer of the API.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
1a. (I) /noun/ A collection of data that is stored for a
relatively long period of time for historical and other purposes,
such as to support audit service, availability service, or system
integrity service. (Compare: backup, repository.)

1b. (I) /verb/ To store data in such a way as to create an
archive. (Compare: back up.)

Tutorial: A digital signature may need to be verified many years
after the signing occurs. The CA -- the one that issued the
certificate containing the public key needed to verify that
signature -- may not stay in operation that long. So every CA
needs to provide for long-term storage of the information needed
to verify the signatures of those to whom it issues certificates.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Noun: See Archive facility.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Verb: To place a cryptographic key and/or metadata into long-term storage that will be maintained even if the storage technology changes.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A facility used for long-term key and/or metadata storage.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Long-term, physically separate storage.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 1. To place information into long-term storage.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 2. A location or media used for long-term storage.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See Key management archive.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 1. To place information into long-term storage. 2. A locat ion or media ussed for long-term storage.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: To place information into long-term storage. Also, see Key management archive.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A function in the lifecycle of keying material; a repository for the long- term storage of keying material.

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: CodePipeline : A copy of the files or changes that are worked on by the pipeline.

definitions.source_name: CPAN Security Group (CPANSec) Glossary
definitions.definition: ( SLSA-2023 ) An immutable blob of data; primarily refers to software , but SLSA can be used for any artifact. E.g. a file, a git commit, a directory of files (serialized in some way), a container image, a firmware image.

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: Any technology system, whether developed internally or obtained from a third party, that utilizes an artificial intelligence technology.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Any data system, software, hardware, application, tool, or utility that operates in whole or in part using AI.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A relationship for a particular purpose. For example, a key is associated with the application or process for which it will be used.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A relationship for a particular purpose; for example, a key is associated with the application or process for which it will be used.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) A cryptographic key that is used in an asymmetric
cryptographic algorithm. (See: asymmetric cryptography, private
key, public key.)

definitions.source_name: Canadian Centre for Cyber Security Glossary
definitions.definition: Two related keys (a public key and a private key) that perform complementary operations, such as encrypt and decrypt or generate signatures.

definitions.source_name: NICCS Cybersecurity Glossary (CISA)
definitions.definition: Attack patterns are standardized descriptions of common methods used by adversaries to exploit software, hardware, or network vulnerabilities, often categorized by TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures) to aid defense.  For software, descriptions of common methods for exploiting software systems.

definitions.source_name: CWE Glossary (also CAPEC, CAR)
definitions.definition: the common approach and attributes related to the exploitation of a weakness in a software, firmware, hardware, or service component.

definitions.source_name: CPAN Security Group (CPANSec) Glossary
definitions.definition: An authenticated statement (metadata) about a software artifact or collection of software artifacts. E.g. a signed SLSA Provenance file.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The issue of a statement, based on a decision, that fulfillment of specified requirements has been demonstrated.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of providing a digital signature for a set of measurements securely stored in hardware, and then having the requester validate the signature and the set of measurements.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (I) Synonym for "security audit trail".

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A chronological record of information system activities, including records of system accesses and operations performed in a given period.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A chronological record of system activities. Includes records of system accesses and operations performed in a given period.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A record providing documentary evidence of specific events.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A chronological record of system activities, including records of system accesses and operations performed in a given period.

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: Encryption that provides confidentiality, data integrity, and authenticity assurances of the encrypted data.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The function of GCM in which the plaintext is encrypted into the ciphertext, and an authentication tag is generated on the AAD and the ciphertext.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(D) Synonym for a checksum based on cryptography. (Compare: Data
Authentication Code, Message Authentication Code.)

Deprecated Term: IDOCs SHOULD NOT use this uncapitalized term as a
synonym for any kind of checksum, regardless of whether or not the
checksum is cryptographic. Instead, use "checksum", "Data
Authentication Code", "error detection code", "hash", "keyed
hash", "Message Authentication Code", "protected checksum", or
some other recommended term, depending on what is meant.

The term mixes concepts in a potentially misleading way. The word
"authentication" is misleading because the checksum may be used to
perform a data integrity function rather than a data origin
authentication function.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A keyed cryptographic checksum based on an approved security function (also known as a Message Authentication Code).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A cryptographic checksum based on an approved security function (also known as a Message Authentication Code).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A keyed cryptographic checksum based on an approved security function; also known as a Message Authentication Code.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) An Internet protocol [R2402, R4302] designed to provide
connectionless data integrity service and connectionless data
origin authentication service for IP datagrams, and (optionally)
to provide partial sequence integrity and protection against
replay attacks. (See: IPsec. Compare: ESP.)

Tutorial: Replay protection may be selected by the receiver when a
security association is established. AH authenticates the upper-
layer PDU that is carried as an IP SDU, and also authenticates as
much of the IP PCI (i.e., the IP header) as possible. However,
some IP header fields may change in transit, and the value of
these fields, when the packet arrives at the receiver, may not be
predictable by the sender. Thus, the values of such fields cannot
be protected end-to-end by AH; protection of the IP header by AH
is only partial when such fields are present.

AH may be used alone, or in combination with the ESP, or in a
nested fashion with tunneling. Security services can be provided
between a pair of communicating hosts, between a pair of
communicating security gateways, or between a host and a gateway.
ESP can provide nearly the same security services as AH, and ESP
can also provide data confidentiality service. The main difference
between authentication services provided by ESP and AH is the
extent of the coverage; ESP does not protect IP header fields
unless they are encapsulated by AH.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A deprecated IPsec security protocol that provides integrity protection (but not confidentiality) for packet headers and data.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) Information used to verify an identity claimed by or for an
entity. (See: authentication, credential, user. Compare:
identification information.)

Tutorial: Authentication information may exist as, or be derived
from, one of the following: (a) Something the entity knows (see:
password); (b) something the entity possesses (see: token); (c)
something the entity is (see: biometric authentication).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Information used to establish the validity of a claimed identity.

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: A defined sequence of messages between a Claimant and a Verifier that demonstrates that the Claimant has possession and control of a valid token to establish their identity, and optionally, demonstrates to the Claimant that they are communicating with the intended Verifier.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 2. A defined sequence of messages between a Claimant and a Verifier that demonstrates that the Claimant has possession and control of a valid token to establish his/her identity, and optionally, demonstrates to the Claimant that he or she is communicating with the intended Verifier.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 1. A well specified message exchange process between a claimant and a verifier that enables the verifier to confirm the claimant’s identity.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A defined sequence of messages between a claimant and a verifier that demonstrates that the claimant has possession and control of one or more valid authenticators to establish their identity, and, optionally, demonstrates that the claimant is communicating with the intended verifier.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A defined sequence of messages between a Claimant and a Verifier that demonstrates that the Claimant has possession and control of a valid token to establish his/her identity, and optionally, demonstrates to the Claimant that he or she is communicating with the intended Verifier.

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: Something the claimant possesses and controls (typically a cryptographic module or password) that is used to authenticate the claimant’s identity (e.g., token).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Something the cardholder possesses and controls (e.g., PIV Card or derived PIV credential) that is used to authenticate the cardholder’s identity.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Authentication using two or more different factors to achieve authentication. Factors include something you know (e.g., PIN, password), something you have (e.g., cryptographic identification device, token), or something you are (e.g., biometric).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An entity that facilitates authentication of other entities attached to the same LAN using a public key certificate.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Something the claimant possesses and controls (typically a cryptographic module or password) that is used to authenticate the claimant’s identity. This was previously referred to as a token.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The means used to confirm the identity of a user, process, or device (e.g., user password or token).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Authentication using two or more factors to achieve authentication. Factors include: (i) something you know (e.g. password/personal identification number (PIN)); (ii) something you have (e.g., cryptographic identification device, token); or (iii) something you are (e.g., biometric). See authenticator.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Something that the claimant possesses and controls (such as a key or password) that is used to authenticate a claim. See cryptographic token.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Something that the Claimant possesses and controls (typically a key or password) that is used to authenticate the Claimant’s identity.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The means used to confirm the identity of a user, processor, or device (e.g., user password or token).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Something the claimant possesses and controls (typically a cryptographic module or password) that is used to authenticate the claimant’s identity. In previous editions of SP 800-63, this was referred to as atoken.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See Authenticator.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A portable, user-controlled, physical device (e.g., smart card or memory stick) used to store cryptographic information and possibly also perform cryptographic functions.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Something the claimant possesses and controls (typically a cryptographic module or password) that is used to authenticate the claimant’s identity.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See Authenticator

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Authentication using two or more factors to achieve authentication. Factors are (i) something you know (e.g., password/personal identification number); (ii) something you have (e.g., cryptographic identification device, token); and (iii) something you are (e.g., biometric).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Something the claimant possesses and controls (typically a cryptographic module or password) that is used to authenticate the claimant’s identity. In previous editions of SP 800-63, this was referred to as a token.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Authentication using two or more different factors to achieve authentication. Factors include something you know (e.g., PIN, password); something you have (e.g., cryptographic identification device, token); or something you are (e.g., biometric). See authenticator.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Something that the claimant possesses and controls (typically a cryptographic module or password) that is used to authenticate the claimant’s identity. This was previously referred to as a token.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A physical object a user possesses and controls that is used to authenticate the user’s identity.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A representation of a particular asset that typically relies on a blockchain or other types of distributed ledgers.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
