(especially physical harm) to its system entities. (Compare:
security.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Expectation that a system does not, under defined conditions, lead to a state in which human life, health, property, or the environment is endangered.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Freedom from conditions that can cause death, injury, occupational illness, damage to or loss of equipment or property, or damage to the environment.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (O) See: special access program.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A program established for a specific class of classified information that imposes safeguarding and access requirements that exceed those normally required for information at the same classification level.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (I) See: subordinate certification authority.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The individual, group, or organization responsible for conducting a security control assessment.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An attack enabled by leakage of information from a physical cryptosystem. Characteristics that could be exploited in a side-channel attack include timing, power consumption, and electromagnetic and acoustic emissions.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Confidence that the supply chain will produce and deliver elements, processes, and information that function as expected.

definitions.source_name: ENISA Glossary of Terms
definitions.definition: Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition. SCADA systems are highly distributed systems used to control geographically dispersed assets, often scattered over thousands of square kilometers.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A generic name for a computerized system that is capable of gathering and processing data and applying operational controls over long distances. Typical uses include power transmission and distribution and pipeline systems. SCADA was designed for the unique communication challenges (e.g., delays, data integrity) posed by the various media that must be used, such as phone lines, microwave, and satellite. Usually shared rather than dedicated.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The ability to support more users, concurrent sessions, and throughput than a single SSL VPN device can typically handle.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: The ability to expand as higher and higher volumes occur due to high volume operations with a parallel engine.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (I) /threat action/ See: secondary definition under "exposure".

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Searching through object residue to acquire data.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (O) See: sensitive compartmented information.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Classified information concerning or derived from intelligence sources, methods, or analytical processes, which is required to be handled within formal access control systems established by the Director of National Intelligence.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Classified information concerning or derived from intelligence sources, methods, or analytical processes, which is required to be handled within formal access control systems established by the Director of National Intelligence.

definitions.source_name: CVE Glossary
definitions.definition: A defined set of products, vulnerabilities, or both for which a CNA has authority and responsibility.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: The sum of the products, services, and results to be provided as a project. (PMBOK 3RD EDITION)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A sequence of instructions, ranging from a simple list of operating system commands to full-blown programming language statements, which can be executed automatically by an interpreter.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: A configuration file that lets users run or execute certain actions.

definitions.source_name: CWE Glossary (also CAPEC, CAR)
definitions.definition: Software Development Lifecycle.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A formal or informal methodology for designing, creating, and maintaining software (including code built into hardware).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The scope of activities associated with a system, encompassing the system’s initiation, development and acquisition, implementation, operation and maintenance, and ultimately its disposal that instigates another system initiation.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
1a. (I) /adjective/ The condition of information being protected
from being known by any system entities except those that are
intended to know it. (See: data confidentiality.)

1b. (I) /noun/ An item of information that is protected thusly.

Usage: This term applies to symmetric keys, private keys, and
passwords.

definitions.source_name: Kubernetes Glossary
definitions.definition:
Stores sensitive information, such as passwords, OAuth tokens, and SSH keys.

Secrets give you more control over how sensitive information is used and reduces the risk of accidental exposure. Secret values are encoded as base64 strings and are stored unencrypted by default, but can be configured to be encrypted at rest . A Pod can reference the Secret in a variety of ways, such as in a volume mount or as an environment variable. Secrets are designed for confidential data and ConfigMaps are designed for non-confidential data.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
1a. (I) A system condition in which the system is in conformance
with the applicable security policy. (Compare: clean system,
transaction.)

1b. (I) /formal model/ A system condition in which no subject can
access any object in an unauthorized manner. (See: secondary
definition under "Bell-LaPadula model".)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Condition in which no subject can access any object in an unauthorized manner.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(N) A protocol consisting of XML-based request and response
message formats for exchanging security information, expressed in
the form of assertions about subjects, between on-line business
partners. [SAML]

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A protocol consisting of XML-based request and response message formats for exchanging security information, expressed in the form of assertions about subjects, between on-line business partners.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A framework for exchanging authentication and authorization information. Security typically involves checking the credentials presented by a party for authentication and authorization. SAML standardizes the representation of these credentials in an XML format called assertions, enhancing the interoperability between disparate applications.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An XML-based security specification developed by the Organization for the Advancement of Structured Information Standards (OASIS) for exchanging authentication (and authorization) information between trusted entities over the Internet. See [SAML].

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) /IPsec/ In an IPsec implementation that operates in a network
node, a database that contains parameters to describe the status
and operation of each of the active security associations that the
node has established with other nodes. Separate inbound and
outbound SADs are needed because of the directionality of IPsec
security associations. [R4301] (Compare: SPD.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A list or table of all IPsec SAs, including those that are still being negotiated.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) A chronological record of system activities that is sufficient
to enable the reconstruction and examination of the sequence of
environments and activities surrounding or leading to an
operation, procedure, or event in a security-relevant transaction
from inception to final results. [NCS04] (See: security audit.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A set of records that collectively provide documentary evidence of processing used to aid in tracing from original transactions forward to related records and reports, and/or backwards from records and reports to their component source transactions.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Data collected and potentially used to facilitate a security audit.

definitions.source_name: California SAM 5300 Technical Definitions (CDT)
definitions.definition: A banner presented on the opening screen that informs users of the security implications of accessing a computer resource.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See security banner (also known as notice and consent banners)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 1. A persistent visible window on a computer monitor that displays the highest level of data accessible during the current session.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 2. The opening screen that informs users of the implications of accessing a computer resource (e.g. consent to monitor).

definitions.source_name: Canadian Centre for Cyber Security Glossary
definitions.definition: A management, operational, or technical high-level security requirement needed for an information system to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its IT assets. Security controls can be applied by using a variety of security solutions that can include security products, security policies, security practices, and security procedures.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A safeguard or countermeasure prescribed for an information system or an organization designed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its information and to meet a set of defined security requirements.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A safeguard or countermeasure prescribed for an information system or an organization designed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its information and to meet a set of defined security requirements.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The safeguards or countermeasures prescribed for an information system or an organization to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the system and its information.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A mechanism designed to address needs as specified by a set of security requirements.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A protective measure against threats.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A protection measure for a system.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A safeguard or countermeasure prescribed for an information system or an organization, which is designed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its information and to meet a set of defined security requirements.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The management, operational, and technical controls (i.e., safeguards or countermeasures) prescribed for a system to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the system, its components, processes, and data.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Safeguards or countermeasures prescribed for an information system or an organization to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the system and its information.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A safeguard or countermeasure prescribed for a system or an organization designed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its information and to meet a set of defined security requirements.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(N) The management, operational, and technical controls
(safeguards or countermeasures) prescribed for an information
system which, taken together, satisfy the specified security
requirements and adequately protect the confidentiality,
integrity, and availability of the system and its information.
[FP199] (See: security architecture.)

definitions.source_name: Texas DIR Cybersecurity Terminology
definitions.definition: The management, operational, and technical controls (i.e., safeguards or countermeasures) prescribed for an information system to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the system and its information.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (I) See: domain.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A set of elements, data, resources, and functions that share a commonality in combinations of (1) roles supported, (2) rules governing their use, and (3) protection needs.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Set of assets and resources subject to a common security policy.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A domain that implements a security policy and is administered by a single authority.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An environment or context that includes a set of system resources and a set of system entities that have the right to access the resources as defined by a common security policy, security model, or security architecture. See Security Domain.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See security domain.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A set of subjects, their information objects, and a common security policy.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A system or subsystem that is under the authority of a single trusted authority. Security domains may be organized (e.g., hierarchically) to form larger domains.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An environment or context that includes a set of system resources and a set of system entities that have the right to access the resources as defined by a common security policy, security model, or security architecture.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A domain within which behaviors, interactions, and outcomes occur and that is defined by a governing security policy. Note: A security domain is defined by rules for users, processes, systems, and services that apply to activity within the domain and activity with similar entities in other domains.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A domain within which behaviors, interactions, and outcomes occur and that is defined by a governing security policy. Note: A security domain is defined by rules for users, processes, systems, and services that apply to activity within the domain and activity with similar entities in other domains.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) An occurrence in a system that is relevant to the security of
the system. (See: security incident.)

Tutorial: The term covers both events that are security incidents
and those that are not. In a CA workstation, for example, a list
of security events might include the following:
-  Logging an operator into or out of the system.
-  Performing a cryptographic operation, e.g., signing a digital
   certificate or CRL.
-  Performing a cryptographic card operation: creation, insertion,
   removal, or backup.
-  Performing a digital certificate lifecycle operation: rekey,
   renewal, revocation, or update.
-  Posting a digital certificate to an X.500 Directory.
-  Receiving a key compromise notification.
-  Receiving an improper certification request.
-  Detecting an alarm condition reported by a cryptographic
   module.
-  Failing a built-in hardware self-test or a software system
   integrity check.

definitions.source_name: California SAM 5300 Technical Definitions (CDT)
definitions.definition: An observable occurrence in a network or system, such as a detected vulnerability, or observed signs of attack, anomalies, and suspicious or inappropriate activities.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) A function in a system that is relevant to the security of the
system; i.e., a system function that must operate correctly to
ensure adherence to the system's security policy.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The capability provided by the system or a system element. The capability may be expressed generally as a concept or specified precisely in requirements.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Cryptographic algorithms, together with modes of operation (if appropriate); for example, block cipher algorithms, digital signature algorithms, asymmetric key-establishment algorithms, message authentication codes, hash functions, or random bit generators. See FIPS 140.6

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Cryptographic algorithms, together with modes of operation (if appropriate); for example, block ciphers, digital signature algorithms, asymmetric key-establishment algorithms, message authentication codes, hash functions, or random bit generators. See FIPS 140.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Cryptographic algorithms, together with modes of operation (if appropriate); for example, block ciphers, digital signature algorithms, asymmetric key-establishment algorithms, message authentication codes, hash functions, or random bit generators; see FIPS 140.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) "The hardware, firmware, and software elements of a trusted
computing base that implement the reference monitor concept. It
must mediate all accesses, be protected from modification, and be
verifiable as correct." [NCS04] (See: kernel, TCB.)

Tutorial: A security kernel is an implementation of a reference
monitor for a given hardware base. [Huff]

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Hardware, firmware, and software elements of a trusted computing base implementing the reference monitor concept. Security kernel must mediate all accesses, be protected from modification, and be verifiable as correct.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) An item of meta-data that designates the value of one or more
security-relevant attributes (e.g., security level) of a system
resource. (See: [R1457]. Compare: security marking.)

Deprecated usage: To avoid confusion, IDOCs SHOULD NOT use
"security label" for "security marking", or vice versa, even
though that is commonly done (including in some national and
international standards that should know better).

Tutorial: Humans and automated security mechanisms use a security
label of a system resource to determine, according to applicable
security policy, how to control access to the resource (and they
affix appropriate, matching security markings to physical
instances of the resource). Security labels are most often used to
support data confidentiality policy, and sometimes used to support
data integrity policy.

As explained in [R1457], the form that is taken by security labels
of a protocol's packets varies depending on the OSIRM layer in
which the protocol operates. Like meta-data generally, a security
label of a data packet may be either explicit (e.g., IPSO) or
implicit (e.g., Alice treats all messages received from Bob as
being labeled "Not For Public Release"). In a connectionless
protocol, every packet might have an explicit label; but in a
connection-oriented protocol, all packets might have the same
implicit label that is determined at the time the connection is
established.

Both classified and unclassified system resources may require a
security label. (See: FOUO.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The means used to associate a set of security attributes with a specific information object as part of the data structure for that object.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Explicit or implicit marking of a data structure or output media associated with an information system representing the FIPS 199 security category, or distribution limitations or handling caveats of the information contained therein.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Information that either identifies an associated parameter or provides information regarding the parameter’s proper protection and use.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See security label.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) The combination of a hierarchical classification level and a
set of non-hierarchical category designations that represents how
sensitive a specified type or item of information is. (See:
dominate, lattice model. Compare: classification level.)

Usage: IDOCs that use this term SHOULD state a definition for it.
The term is usually understood to involve sensitivity to
disclosure, but it also is used in many other ways and could
easily be misunderstood.

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: The degree of trust that is associated with a user account, based upon Identification method; one of the attributes of a user account.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) A physical marking that is bound to an instance of a system
resource and that represents a security label of the resource,
i.e., that names or designates the value of one or more security-
relevant attributes of the resource. (Compare: security label.)

Tutorial: A security label may be represented by various
equivalent markings depending on the physical form taken by the
labeled resource. For example, a document could have a marking
composed of a bit pattern [FP188] when the document is stored
electronically as a file in a computer, and also a marking of
printed alphabetic characters when the document is in paper form.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The means used to associate a set of security attributes with objects in a human-readable form, to enable organizational process-based enforcement of information security policies.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See security marking.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The means used to associate a set of security attributes with objects in a human-readable form in order to enable organizational, process-based enforcement of information security policies.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) A method or process (or a device incorporating it) that can be
used in a system to implement a security service that is provided
by or within the system. (See: Tutorial under "security policy".
Compare: security doctrine.)

Usage: Usually understood to refer primarily to components of
communication security, computer security, and emanation security.

Examples: Authentication exchange, checksum, digital signature,
encryption, and traffic padding.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A process or system that is used to produce a particular result.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The fundamental processes involved in or responsible for an action, reaction, or other natural phenomenon.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A natural or established process by which something takes place or is brought about.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A device or method for achieving a security-relevant purpose.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A device or function designed to provide one or more security services usually rated in terms of strength of service and assurance of the design.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A process or system that is used to produce a particular result. The fundamental processes involved in or responsible for an action, reaction, or other natural phenomenon. A natural or established process by which something takes place or is brought about. Refer to security mechanism. Note: A mechanism can be technology- or nontechnology-based (e.g., apparatus, device, instrument, procedure, process, system, operation, method, technique, means, or medium).

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A method, tool, or procedure that is the realization of security requirements. Note 1: A security mechanism exists in machine, technology, human, and physical forms. Note 2: A security mechanism reflects security and trust principles. Note 3: A security mechanism may enforce security policy and therefore must have capabilities consistent with the intent of the security policy.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A process or system that is used to produce a particular result. The fundamental processes involved in or responsible for an action, reaction, or other natural phenomenon. A natural or established process by which something takes place or is brought about. Refer to security mechanism. Note: A mechanism can be technology- or nontechnology-based (e.g., apparatus, device, instrument, procedure, process, system, operation, method, technique, means, or medium).

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
1. (I) /IPsec/ A 32-bit identifier used to distinguish among
security associations that terminate at the same destination (IP
address) and use the same security protocol (AH or ESP). Carried
in AH and ESP to enable the receiving system to determine under
which security association to process a received packet.

2. (I) /mobile IP/ A 32-bit index identifying a security
association from among the collection of associations that are
available between a pair of nodes, for application to mobile IP
protocol messages that the nodes exchange.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Arbitrarily chosen value that acts as a unique identifier for an IPsec connection.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An arbitrarily chosen value that acts as a unique identifier for an IPsec connection.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) A physical or logical boundary that is defined for a domain or
enclave and within which a particular security policy or security
architecture applies. (See: insider, outsider.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Identifies the information resources covered by an accreditation decision, as distinguished from separately accredited information resources that are interconnected or with which information is exchanged via messaging.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: For the purposes of identifying the Protection Level for confidentiality of a system to be accredited, the system has a conceptual boundary that extends to all intended users of the system, both directly and indirectly connected, who receive output from the system.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: All components of an information system to be accredited by an authorizing official and excludes separately accredited systems, to which the information system is connected. Synonymous with the term security perimeter defined in CNSS Instruction 4009 and DCID 6/3.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: All components of an information system to be accredited by an authorizing official and excludes separately accredited systems to which the information system is connected. Synonymous with the term security perimeter defined in CNSS Instruction 4009 and DCID 6/3.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A physical or logical boundary that is defined for a system, domain, or enclave; within which a particular security policy or security architecture is applied.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See Accreditation Boundary.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) /IPsec/ In an IPsec implementation operating in a network
node, a database that contains parameters that specify policies
set by a user or administrator to determine what IPsec services,
if any, are to be provided to IP datagrams sent or received by the
node, and in what fashion they are provided. For each datagram,
the SPD specifies one of three choices: discard the datagram,
apply IPsec services (e.g., AH or ESP), or bypass IPsec. Separate
inbound and outbound SPDs are needed because of the directionality
of IPsec security associations. [R4301] (Compare: SAD.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A prioritized list of all IPsec policies.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(N) /Common Criteria/ A set of security requirements and
specifications to be used as the basis for evaluation of an
identified TOE.

Tutorial: A security target (ST) is a statement of security claims
for a particular information technology security product or
system, and is the basis for agreement among all parties as to
what security the product or system offers. An ST parallels the
structure of a protection profile, but has additional elements
that include product-specific detailed information. An ST contains
a summary specification, which defines the specific measures taken
in the product or system to meet the security requirements.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An implementation-dependent statement of security needs for a specific identified target of evaluation (TOE).

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (I) A value that is an input to a pseudorandom number generator.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A bit string used as input to a pseudorandom process.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The input to a pseudorandom number generator. Different seeds generate different pseudorandom sequences.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A string of bits that is used to initialize a DRBG. Also just called a Seed.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that is used to initialize a process (e.g., a DRBG). Also see RBG seed.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Noun : A string of bits that is used as input to a DRBG mechanism. The seed will determine a portion of the internal state of the DRBG, and its entropy must be sufficient to support the security strength of the DRBG. Verb : To acquire bits with sufficient entropy for the desired security strength. These bits will be used as input to a DRBG mechanism to determine a portion of the initial internal state. Also see reseed.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A string of bits that is used to initialize a DRBG. Also called a Seed.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that is used to initialize a process (e.g., a deterministic random bit generator). Also see RNG seed.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) A public-key certificate for which the public key bound by the
certificate and the private key used to sign the certificate are
components of the same key pair, which belongs to the signer.
(Compare: root certificate.)

Tutorial: In a self-signed X.509 public-key certificate, the
issuer's DN is the same as the subject's DN.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A public-key certificate whose digital signature may be verified by the public key contained within the certificate. The signature on a self-signed certificate protects the integrity of the data, but does not guarantee the authenticity of the information. The trust of self-signed certificates is based on the secure procedures used to distribute them.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A public-key certificate whose digital signature may be verified by the public key contained within the certificate. The signature on a self-signed certificate protects the integrity of the information within the certificate but does not guarantee the authenticity of that information. The trust of self-signed certificates is based on the secure procedures used to distribute them.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A public-key certificate whose digital signature may be verified by the public key contained within the certificate. The signature on a self- signed certificate protects the integrity of the data, but does not guarantee the authenticity of the information. The trust of self-signed certificates is based on the secure procedures used to distribute them.

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: The person or entity sending an email message.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The party that sends secret keying material to the receiver in a key-transport transaction. Contrast with receiver.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The party that sends secret keying material to the receiver using a key-transport transaction. Contrast with receiver.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(O) /U.S. Government/ Classified information concerning or derived
from intelligence sources, methods, or analytical processes, which
is required to be handled within formal control systems
established by the Director of Central Intelligence. [C4009] (See:
compartment, SAP, SCIF. Compare: collateral information.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Classified information concerning or derived from intelligence sources, methods, or analytical processes, which is required to be handled within formal access control systems established by the Director of National Intelligence.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Classified information concerning or derived from intelligence sources, methods, or analytical processes, which is required to be handled within formal access control systems established by the Director of National Intelligence.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 1. A subset of Classified National Intelligence concerning or derived from intelligence sources, methods, or analytical processes, that is required to be protected within formal access control systems established by the Director of National Intelligence.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 2. Classified information concerning or derived from intelligence sources, methods, or analytical processes, which is required to be handled within formal access control systems established by the Director of National Intelligence.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(O) /U.S. Government/ "An accredited area, room, group of rooms,
building, or installation where SCI may be stored, used,
discussed, and/or processed." [C4009] (See: SCI. Compare: shielded
enclosure.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An area, room, group of rooms, buildings, or installation certified and accredited as meeting Director of National Intelligence security standards for the processing, storage, and/or discussion of sensitive compartmented information (SCI).

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: AWS Service Catalog is a web service that helps organizations create and manage catalogs of IT services that are approved for use on AWS. These IT services can include everything from virtual machine images, servers, software, and databases to complete multitier application architectures. See also https://aws.amazon.com/servicecatalog/ .

definitions.source_name: Kubernetes Glossary
definitions.definition:
A former extension API that enabled applications running in Kubernetes clusters to easily use external managed software offerings, such as a datastore service offered by a cloud provider.

It provided a way to list, provision, and bind with external Managed Services without needing detailed knowledge about how those services would be created or managed.

definitions.source_name: Washington State AG Data Breach Glossary
definitions.definition: Exploiting a valid computer session to gain unauthorized access to information or services in a computer system.

definitions.source_name: INCIBE Glosario de terminos de ciberseguridad
definitions.definition: También llamado secuestro de cookies, es un ataque basado en interceptar la sesión de un usuario en Internet para acceder a su información o servicios sin autorización. Se suele dar en sesiones no cifradas como las HTTP. Este tipo de ataque se ayuda de varias técnicas como Man-in-the-Middle o XSS (cross site scripting) para lograr su objetivo, así como de programas de malware específicos para robar cookies de sesión.

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: Secure Hash Algorithm. SHA1 is an earlier version of the algorithm, which AWS has replaced with SHA256.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A hash algorithm with the property that it is computationally infeasible 1) to find a message that corresponds to a given message digest, or 2) to find two different messages that produce the same message digest.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Secure Hash Algorithm.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(D) Synonym for "cryptographic key" or "password".

Deprecated Usage: IDOCs that use this term SHOULD state a
definition for it because the term is used in many ways and could
easily be misunderstood.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The secret byte string that is computed/generated during the execution of an approved key-establishment scheme and used as input to a key-derivation method as part of that transaction.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that has been computed during a key-establishment scheme, is known by both participants, and is used as input to a key-derivation method to produce keying material.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that has been computed using a key-establishment scheme and is used as input to a (single-step) key derivation function or an extraction-then-expansion procedure that derives keying material in a two-step process.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that has been computed using a key agreement algorithm.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that has been computed using a key-agreement scheme and is used as input to a key derivation method.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A value generated during a public-key-based key establishment scheme defined in NIST SP 800-56A or SP 800-56B.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that has been computed using a key-agreement scheme and is used as input to a key-derivation function/method.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret used in authentication that is known to the subscriber and the verifier.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that has been computed during the execution of a key-establishment scheme, is known by both participants, and is used as input to a key-derivation method to produce secret keying material.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that has been computed during an execution of a key-establishment scheme between two parties, is known by both participants, and is used as input to a key-derivation method to produce keying material.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that is computed during a (pair-wise) key-agreement transaction and is used as input to derive a key using a key-derivation method.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that has been computed using a key-agreement scheme and is used as input to a key-derivation method.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret value that has been computed during a key-establishment scheme, is known by both participants, and is used as input to a key-derivation method to produce keying material.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A secret used in authentication that is known to the Claimant and the Verifier.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(O) "Room or container designed to attenuate electromagnetic
radiation, acoustic signals, or emanations." [C4009] (See:
emanation. Compare: SCIF.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Room or container designed to attenuate electromagnetic radiation, acoustic signals, or emanations.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(O) "Identifying combination of letters and numbers assigned to
certain items of COMSEC material to facilitate handling,
accounting, and controlling." [C4009] (Compare: KMID, long title.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Identifying combination of letters and numbers assigned to certain COMSEC materials to facilitate handling, accounting, and controlling (e.g., KAM-211, KG-175). Each item of accountable COMSEC material is assigned a short title to facilitate handling, accounting and control.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) A public-key certificate that contains a public key that is
intended to be used for verifying digital signatures, rather than
for encrypting data or performing other cryptographic functions.

Tutorial: A v3 X.509 public-key certificate may have a "keyUsage"
extension that indicates the purpose for which the certified
public key is intended. (See: certificate profile.)

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A public key certificate that contains a public key intended for verifying digital signatures rather than authenticating, encrypting data or performing any other cryptographic functions.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A public key certificate that contains a public key intended for verifying digital signatures rather than encrypting data or performing any other cryptographic functions.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(N) A human being or organization entity that uses a private key
to sign (i.e., create a digital signature on) a data object. [DSG]

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: AWS Signer is a fully managed code-signing service used to ensure the authenticity and integrity of an AWS customer's code.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) A (usually) UDP-based, Application-Layer, Internet Standard
protocol (RFCs 3410-3418) for conveying management information
between system components that act as managers and agents.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: General: The Internet standard protocol, defined in STD 15, RFC 1157, developed to manage nodes on an IP network. It is a simple and expandable protocol designed to give the capability to remotely manage a computer network by polling, setting terminal values, and monitoring network events. It is comprised of three elements, an MIB, a manager, and the agents. The manager is located on the host computer on the network. Its role is to poll the agents and request information concerning the networks status. Agents run off each network node and collect network and terminal information as specified in the MIB. Enterprise Architecture: Exposes network data in the form of variables on the managed systems organized in a management information base (MIB), which describe the system status and configuration. These variables can then be remotely queried by managing applications.

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: See SOAP .

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An XML-based protocol for exchanging structured information in a decentralized, distributed environment.

definitions.source_name: NICCS Cybersecurity Glossary (CISA)
definitions.definition: Comprehending information about the current and developing security posture and risks, based on information gathered, observation and analysis, and knowledge or experience.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Perception of elements in the system and/or environment and a comprehension of their meaning, which could include a projection of the future status of perceived elements and the uncertainty associated with that status.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Within a volume of time and space, the perception of an enterprise’s security posture and its threat environment; the comprehension/meaning of both taken together (risk); and the projection of their status into the near future.

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: A mobile phone that performs many of the functions of a computer, typically having a touchscreen interface, internet access, and an operating system capable of running downloaded applications. The use of a smartphone requires a data plan.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: A class of smart devices that combines cellular and mobile computing functions into one unit. They are distinguished from feature phones by their stronger hardware capabilities and extensive mobile operating systems, which facilitate wider software, internet (including web browsing over mobile broadband), and multimedia functionality (including music, video, cameras, and gaming), alongside core phone functions such as voice calls and text messaging. Smartphones typically contain a number of metal–oxide–semiconductor (MOS) integrated circuit (IC) chips, include various sensors that can be leveraged by pre-included and third-party software (such as a magnetometer, proximity sensors, barometer, gyroscope, accelerometer and more), and support wireless communications protocols such as: Bluetooth; Wi-Fi; or satellite navigation.

definitions.source_name: ENISA Glossary of Terms
definitions.definition: Small and Medium Enterprise

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: Subject Matter Expert

definitions.source_name: NICCS Cybersecurity Glossary (CISA)
definitions.definition: the fraudulent practice of sending text messages purporting to be from reputable companies in order to induce individuals to reveal personal information

definitions.source_name: NCSC UK Glossary
definitions.definition: Combination of 'SMS' and 'Phishing'. Untargeted text messages sent to large numbers of people, asking for sensitive information, or encouraging them to visit a fake website.

definitions.source_name: INCIBE Glosario de terminos de ciberseguridad
definitions.definition:
Un sniffer es un programa que monitoriza la información que circula por la red con el objeto de capturar información.

Las tarjetas de red pueden verificar si la información recibida está dirigida o no a su sistema.

Si no es así, la rechaza. Un sniffer lo que hace es colocar a la placa de red en un modo el cual desactiva el filtro de verificación de direcciones (promiscuo) y por lo tanto acepta todos los paquetes que llegan a la tarjeta de red del ordenador donde está instalado estén dirigidos o no a ese dispositivo.

El tráfico que no viaje cifrado podrá por tanto ser «escuchado» por el usuario del sniffer.

El análisis de tráfico puede ser utilizado también para determinar relaciones entre varios usuarios (conocer con qué usuarios o sistemas se relaciona alguien en concreto).

No es fácil detectar si nuestro tráfico de red está siendo «escuchado» mediante un sniffer, por lo que siempre es recomendable utilizar tráfico cifrado en todas las comunicaciones.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: See packet sniffer and passive wiretapping.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(D) /slang/ Synonym for "passive wiretapping"; most often refers
to capturing and examining the data packets carried on a LAN.
(See: password sniffing.)

Deprecated Term: IDOCs SHOULD NOT use this term; it unnecessarily
duplicates the meaning of a term that is better established. (See:
Deprecated Usage under "Green Book".

definitions.source_name: Texas DIR Cybersecurity Terminology
definitions.definition: A passive technique that monitors network communication, decodes protocols, and examines headers and payloads for information of interest. It is both a review technique and a target identification and analysis technique.

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: See "Service Oriented Architecture"

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A set of principles and methodologies for designing and developing software in the form of interoperable services. These services are well-defined business functions that are built as software components (i.e., discrete pieces of code and/or data structures) that can be reused for different purposes.

definitions.source_name: Glossário de Segurança da Informação (GSI/PR Brazil)
definitions.definition: desenvolvido pelo American Institute of CPAs (AICPA), define critérios para gerenciamento de dados dos usuários, baseados nos cinco princípios de confiança do serviço - disponibilidade, integridade, confidencialidade, segurança e privacidade - sendo considerado um requisito mínimo a ser atendido pelo provedor de serviço de nuvem. O relatório tipo I informa se o projeto dos sistemas do provedor de serviço de nuvem é adequado para atender os princípios de confiança relevantes. O relatório tipo II detalha a efetividade operacional dos sistemas do provedor de serviço de nuvem;

definitions.source_name: FedRAMP Terminology Glossary
definitions.definition:
Developed by the American Institute of CPAs (AICPA), the SOC 2 framework includes five key sections, forming a set of criteria called the Trust Services Principles. These include:
1. The security of the service provider’s system
2. The processing integrity of this system
3. The availability of this system
4. The privacy of personal information that the service provider collects, retains, uses, discloses and disposes of for user entities
5. The confidentiality of the information that the service provider’s system processes or maintains for user entities

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: A type of procurement in which only one vendor can supply the required commodity or service and where the state agency has awarded a contract to that one vendor.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: A product or service which is practicably available only from one source. (DGS – APSPM)

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(D) Synonym for "data origin authentication" or "peer entity
authentication". (See: data origin authentication, peer entity
authentication).

Deprecated Term: IDOCs SHOULD NOT use this term because it is
ambiguous and, in either meaning, duplicates the meaning of
internationally standardized terms. If the intent is to
authenticate the original creator or packager of data received,
then use "data origin authentication". If the intent is to
authenticate the identity of the sender of data in the current
instance, then use "peer entity authentication".

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A process that provides assurance of the source of information.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of providing assurance about the source of information. Sometimes called identity authentication or origin authentication.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of providing assurance about the source of information. Sometimes called origin authentication. Compare with Entity authentication.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of providing assurance about the source of information; sometimes called data-origin authentication. Compare with Identity authentication.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The process of providing assurance about the source of information. Sometimes called origin authentication. Compare with Identity authentication.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (I) See: Security Policy Database.

definitions.source_name: ENISA Glossary of Terms
definitions.definition: Single programming document

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition: (I) See: Security Parameters Index.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Arbitrarily chosen value that acts as a unique identifier for an IPsec connection.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
1. (I) A security technique in which two or more entities
separately hold data items that individually do not convey
knowledge of the information that results from combining the
items. (See: dual control, split key.)

2. (O) "A condition under which two or more entities separately
have key components [that] individually convey no knowledge of the
plaintext key [that] will be produced when the key components are
combined in the cryptographic module." [FP140]

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 2. A process by which a cryptographic key is split into multiple key components, individually sharing no knowledge of the original key, which can be subsequently input into, or output from, a cryptographic module by separate entities and combined to recreate the original cryptographic key.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: 1. Separation of data or information into two or more parts, each part constantly kept under control of separate authorized individuals or teams so that no one individual or team will know the whole data.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A process by which a cryptographic key is split into n key components, each of which provides no knowledge of the original key. The components can be subsequently combined to recreate the original cryptographic key. If knowledge of k (where k is less than or equal to n) components is required to construct the original key, then knowledge of any k – 1 key components provides no information about the original key other than, possibly, its length. Note that in this Recommendation, split knowledge is not intended to cover key shares, such as those used in threshold or multi-party signatures.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A process by which a cryptographic key is split into n key shares, each of which provides no knowledge of the key. The shares can be subsequently combined to create or recreate a cryptographic key or to perform independent cryptographic operations on the data to be protected using each key share. If knowledge of k (where k is less than or equal to n) shares is required to construct the key, then knowledge of any k – 1 key shares provides no information about the key other than, possibly, its length.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A process by which a cryptographic key is split into n multiple key components, individually providing no knowledge of the original key, which can be subsequently combined to recreate the original cryptographic key. If knowledge of k (where k is less than or equal to n) components is required to construct the original key, then knowledge of any k-1 key components provides no information about the original key other than, possibly, its length. Note that in this document, split knowledge is not intended to cover key shares, such as those used in threshold or multi-party signatures.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(N) A TRANSEC technique that transmits a signal in a bandwidth
much greater than the transmitted information needs. [F1037]
Example: frequency hopping.

Tutorial: Usually uses a sequential, noise-like signal structure
to spread the normally narrowband information signal over a
relatively wide band of frequencies. The receiver correlates the
signals to retrieve the original information signal. This
technique decreases potential interference to other receivers,
while achieving data confidentiality and increasing immunity of
spread spectrum receivers to noise and interference.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Telecommunications techniques in which a signal is transmitted in a bandwidth considerably greater than the frequency content of the original information. Frequency hopping, direct sequence spreading, time scrambling, and combinations of these techniques are forms of spread spectrum.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Individual or organization having a right, share, claim, or interest in a system or in its possession of characteristics that meet their needs and expectations.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Individual, team, organization, or classes thereof, having an interest in a system.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: Persons and organizations such as customers, sponsors, performing organization and the public, that are actively involved in the project, or whose interests may be positively or negatively affected by execution or completion of the project. PMBOK

definitions.source_name: ENISA Glossary of Terms
definitions.definition: Guidance set up and established by authority as a rule for the measure of quantity, weight, extent, value, or quality.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: Are specific and, where applicable, technical documents containing directives and mandatory specifications governing the management, development, and use of information technology resources. (COV ITRM STANDARD GOV2000-01.1)

definitions.source_name: California SAM 5300 Technical Definitions (CDT)
definitions.definition: Any entity within the executive branch that is under the direct authority of the Governor, including, but not limited to, all agencies, departments, boards, bureaus, commissions, councils, institutions, offices, or other distinct governmental organization not specifically exempted from adherence to the legal and regulatory requirements related to information security and privacy set forth herein.

definitions.source_name: NY State ITS Glossary
definitions.definition: Any entity that falls within the definition of "State Government" entities as defined in Executive Order 117 or "State Agencies" as defined in Section 101 of the State Technology Law.

definitions.source_name: AWS Glossary Reference
definitions.definition: A place at an AWS facility where your AWS Import/Export data is transferred on to, or off of, your storage device.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A client device in a wireless network.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: Retrievable retention of data. Electronic, electrostatic, or electrical hardware or other elements (media) into which data may be entered, and from which data may be retrieved.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: The holding of data in an electromagnetic form for access by a computer processor. Primary storage is data in random access memory (RAM) and other “built-in” devices. Secondary storage is data on hard disks, tapes, and other external devices.

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
1. (I) An authentication process that uses a cryptographic
security mechanism -- particularly public-key certificates -- to
verify the identity claimed for an entity. (Compare: simple
authentication.)

2. (O) "Authentication by means of cryptographically derived
credentials." [X509]

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A method used to secure computer systems and/or networks by verifying a user’s identity by requiring two-factors in order to authenticate (something you know, something you are, or something you have).

definitions.source_name: Washington State AG Data Breach Glossary
definitions.definition: SQL (pronounced “sequel”) is a standardized programming language that is used to manage relational databases —a type of database that organizes data into rows and columns, which collectively form a table— and perform various operations on the data in them.

definitions.source_name: COV IT Glossary (Virginia)
definitions.definition: An industry-standard language for creating, updating, and querying relational database management systems.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: A field in an X.509 certificate that identifies one or more fully qualified domain names, IP addresses, email addresses, URIs, or UPNs to be associated with the public key contained in a certificate.

definitions.source_name: Kubernetes Glossary
definitions.definition:
Subject Alternative Name is an X.509 certificate extension that allows identities to be bound to the subject of the certificate.

The standard defines identities represented as an email address, a DNS name, an IP address or a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI).

definitions.source_name: RFC 4949 - Internet Security Glossary v2
definitions.definition:
(I) /PKI/ A user that is registered in a PKI and, therefore, can
be named in the "subject" field of a certificate issued by a CA in
that PKI. (See: registration, user.)

Usage: This term is needed to distinguish registered users from
two other kinds of PKI users:
-  Users that access the PKI but are not identified to it: For
   example, a relying party may access a PKI repository to obtain
   the certificate of some other party. (See: access.)
-  Users that do not access the PKI: For example, a relying party
   (see: certificate user) may use a digital certificate that was
   obtained from a database that is not part of the PKI that
   issued the certificate.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An entity that has applied for and received a certificate from a Certificate Authority.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: An entity that (1) is the subject named or identified in a certificate issued to such an entity, and (2) holds a private key that corresponds to a public key listed in that certificate.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
definitions.definition: The individual who is the subject named or identified in a Derived PIV Authentication certificate and who holds the token that contains the private key that corresponds to the public key in the certificate.

definitions.source_name: NIST Computer Security Resource Center (CSRC) Glossary — consolidated terminology from NIST cybersecurity publications
